file.content:
# Andes helper size optimization plan

Audit of `andes_*.s` (top-level, not rv32e) for size-reducing edits.
Execute one item per commit. After each: `make clean && make && make size-all`
and run the mapped `make test-*` targets. All must PASS before committing.

Patterns mirror prior commits on `s_*.s`:
- c40cb6ed9 dead `mul`/`mulhu` removal
- b672cb2f9 hoist duplicate `li` constants
- 026bf3e50 `li 0; sw` -> `sw x0`
- f21da4f44 swap `__adddf3` args to kill `mv` pair

---

## Tier A - trivial, mechanical (start here)

### [A1] andes_0.s - fold adjacent `addi sp, sp, -16` pairs
- Two pairs in qzero prologue+epilogue (~lines 219-220, matching epilogue)
- Two pairs in pzero prologue+epilogue (~lines 235-236, matching epilogue)
- Each pair collapses to single `addi sp, sp, -32` / `+32`
- Saves ~4 instructions / 16 bytes
- Verify: `make size-all` (no test target covers j0/y0)

### [A2] andes_1.s - same as A1
- Same pattern in qone/pone
- Saves ~4 instructions / 16 bytes
- Verify: `make size-all`

### [A3] andes_n.s - fold adjacent sp adjusts (investigate first)
- Lines ~53-54 and ~101-102 have two adjacent `addi sp, sp, 16`
- Line 102 source comment says `#8` but instruction is `16`. Check git blame
  to confirm whether the comment is stale or there is a latent bug. If bug,
  fix it as separate commit before folding.
- After confirmation, collapse adjacent pairs
- Saves ~2 instructions / 8 bytes
- Verify: `make size-all`

### [A4] andes_fpexsqrt.s - remove 3 dead `mul` instructions
- Lines 48-49: `mulhu x15, x13, x9; mul x14, x13, x9` - x14 killed before read
- Lines 51-52: `mulhu x15, x9, x18; mul x14, x9, x18` - x14 killed before read
- Lines 53-54: `mulhu x11, x15, x10; mul x10, x15, x10` - x10 overwritten
- Verify each dead-ness by 10-line window read before editing
- Saves ~3 instructions / 12 bytes in a 71-line file (largest relative win)
- Verify: `make test-sqrtf`

---

## Tier B - dead mul removals + li hoists (one commit per file)

### [B1] andes_fpexatan.s
- Dead muls at ~145-146, ~212-213, ~228-229, ~240-241 (verify each)
- Duplicate `li x15, 2147483648` at ~86, 155, 270 - hoist if liveness allows
- Saves ~4-7 instructions
- Verify: `make test-atanf test-asinf test-acosf`

### [B2] andes_dpexsqrt.s
- Dead muls in even/odd exponent arms (lines 18-20, 32-34)
- Duplicate `li x9, 65535` at lines 55, 97, 116, 135 - hoist if x9 is free
  across the span
- Saves ~4-7 instructions
- Verify: `make test-sqrt`

### [B3] andes_fpexlog.s
- Dead muls at ~162-166, ~174-175 (verify each)
- Duplicate `li x21, 2147483648` at ~71, 82, 211, 261 - hoist candidate
- Epilogue splits into 32+16-byte pops; combine if safe
- Saves ~4-8 instructions
- Verify: `make test-logf test-log10f test-powf`

### [B4] andes_dpexatan.s
- Dead mul at ~175-178
- Duplicate `li ..., 2147483648` loads - hoist candidates
- Saves ~2-5 instructions
- Verify: `make test-atan test-asin test-acos`

### [B5] andes_fpexexp.s
- Dead mul at ~75-78 (`mul x10, x18, x10` then `mv x10, x11`)
- Saves ~1 instruction
- Verify: `make test-expf test-coshf test-sinhf test-tanhf test-powf`

---

## Tier C - scaffolding factoring (higher risk, do last)

### [C1] andes_dpexlog.s - factor duplicate `__clzsi2` scaffolds
- Two identical 14-line push/call/pop blocks around __clzsi2 (lines ~119-133
  and ~207-221)
- Collapse into a single in-file helper label + two calls
- Saves ~10-12 instructions if done cleanly
- Verify: `make test-log test-log10 test-pow`

### [C2] andes_fpexlog.s - factor duplicate `__clzsi2` scaffolds
- Same pattern as C1 applied to fpexlog
- Saves ~10 instructions
- Verify: `make test-logf test-log10f test-powf`

### [C3] andes_dpexexp.s - factor duplicate `__gf_sigmax` call blocks
- Two ~24-line save+call+restore sequences differing only in table address
  (expcoo vs expcon), lines ~129-152 and ~154-176
- Refactor to load table pointer before a shared `call`/`tail` block
- Saves ~18-20 instructions - largest single-file win
- Verify: `make test-exp test-cosh test-sinh test-tanh test-pow`

---

## Skipped (not worth it)

- andes_dpreduct.s / andes_fpreduct.s - carry-chain code already minimal for
  ADC-less RISC-V
- andes_r.s / andes_r4.s - dispatcher code already tight
- andes_up4.s .. andes_up8.s - each chains to the next; tightest possible
- andes_p4.s .. andes_p8ac.s - prologue/epilogue minimal
- Structural duplication of div/remu loops in dpexsqrt / fpexatan / dpexlog -
  large potential, high correctness risk, defer indefinitely

---

## Verification checklist (per commit)

1. Read 10-line window around each edit to confirm register liveness.
2. `make clean && make` - must build cleanly.
3. `make size-all > /tmp/after.txt` and diff vs baseline to confirm shrink.
4. `make test-<f>` for every mapped function - every one must `PASS`.
5. Commit via `/commit` (commit-optimizer skill) to capture size delta.
6. If any test FAILs: revert, re-read, do not batch-fix.

## Baseline capture (do once before starting)

```
make clean && make
make size-all > /tmp/andes-size-baseline.txt
```

content.text:
Now I understand all three files. Let me implement each C task carefully.

**C1 analysis (dpexlog):** Two 15-instruction `__clzsi2` scaffold blocks at lines 119-133 (arg in x19) and 207-221 (arg in x13). Factor into local helper taking arg via x15.

**C2 analysis (fpexlog):** Block 1 (lines 84-99, 16 instr, arg already in x10) and Block 2 (lines 215-231, 17 instr, arg in x15 via mv x10,x15). Use x17 as input register to helper - avoids x10 restore conflicts.

**C3 analysis (dpexexp):** Two 23-instruction `__gf_sigmax` blocks differing only in table address (expcoo vs expcon, lines 129-152 and 154-176). Hoist `sw x14` and `la x14,<table>` out, share 21-instruction common body.

Implementing C1 first:

	.size	__gf_dpexlog, .Ltmp0-__gf_dpexlog
# Local helper: call __clzsi2 with arg in x15, preserves x10-x14, result in x15.
.Ldo_clzsi2:
	addi	sp, sp, -24
	sw	ra, 20(sp)
	sw	x10, 0(sp)
	sw	x11, 4(sp)
	sw	x12, 8(sp)
	sw	x13, 12(sp)
	sw	x14, 16(sp)
	mv	x10, x15
	call	__clzsi2
	mv	x15, x10
	lw	x10, 0(sp)
	lw	x11, 4(sp)

content.text:
C1 saves 950 - 624 = **326 bytes** from the `.o` file (text section). That's larger than my instruction count estimate because the helper is also part of the text. Wait, 326 bytes = 81.5 instructions? Let me recalculate:

- Original blocks: 15+15 = 30 instructions = 120 bytes
- New callsites: 2+2 = 4 instructions = 16 bytes
- Helper: 18 instructions = 72 bytes
- Net savings: 120 - (16 + 72) = 32 bytes

But we're seeing 326 bytes difference. Something is wrong - perhaps the git HEAD already has a different version of the file, or the linker is doing something. Actually wait - the git show is getting the current committed version which already has the B-tier optimizations from prior commits. Let me check.

# Makefile for libm accuracy test — A/B comparison
# Target: RV32IM or RV64IM soft-float (Andes optimized assembly)
#
# Usage:
#   make size-all       - report .text size for each assembly function
#   make size-sinf      - report .text size for a single function
#   make test-all       — build, run opt vs ref in QEMU, diff outputs
#   make test-sinf      — test a single function
#
# Toolchain selection:
#   make XLEN=32 ...    - use RV32 toolchain (default)
#   make XLEN=64 ...    - use RV64 toolchain

XLEN ?= 32

ifeq ($(XLEN),64)
  TOOLCHAIN  = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds64le-elf-mculib-v5
  PREFIX     = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv64-elf-
  ARCH       = -march=rv64imc -mabi=lp64
  QEMU       = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv64
  QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-ax25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios
else
  TOOLCHAIN  = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5
  PREFIX     = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv32-elf-
  ARCH       = -march=rv32imc -mabi=ilp32
  QEMU       = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
  QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-n25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios
endif

CC  = $(PREFIX)gcc

content.content: Launching skill: commit-optimizer

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/nick/.claude/skills/commit-optimizer

# Commit Optimizer Skill

Creates well-documented commit messages for optimization work with automatic benchmarking.

## Usage

```
/commit [problem="<description>"] [approach="<description>"]
```

## How It Works

1. **Validates optimization context** — checks that you're in an optimization commit (staged changes present)
2. **Detects what changed** — examines git diff to understand which functions/files were modified
3. **Runs benchmarks** — executes the project's measurement tools (Makefile, commit-size script, or QEMU tests) to capture before/after metrics
4. **Builds commit message** — combines problem statement, approach, and measured results into a structured message

## Commit Message Format

The skill generates messages in this format:

```
optimize: <one-line summary>

Problem:
<explanation of what was slow/large and why it mattered>

Approach:
<description of the optimization technique used>

Result:
<measured improvement with specific metrics>
```

## Examples

**Example 1: Assembly leaf function optimization**
```
optimize: sf_floor.s as leaf function (remove ra push/pop)

Problem:
sf_floor saved and restored return address (ra) unnecessarily, adding 2 instructions (~8 bytes) and extra memory traffic on every call.

Approach:
Eliminated ra push/pop by using tail-call pattern; function now only uses caller-saved registers and doesn't need to preserve ra.

Result:
Code size: 24 → 16 bytes (-33%)
No performance loss (leaf function overhead removed)
```

**Example 2: Register allocation tuning**
```
optimize: sinf.s improve register allocation for FPU operations

Problem:
Register spills were causing extra load/store operations in the hot path of sinf, adding latency on FPU-heavy workloads.

Approach:
Reordered argument reduction to keep intermediate values in registers longer; used callee-saved registers more strategically to reduce spill pressure.

Result:
Code size: 312 → 298 bytes (-4%)
Execution time: 142 → 136 cycles (-4%)
```

## What the Skill Does

- **Auto-detects function** from changed files (e.g., `sf_floor.s` → optimizing `sf_floor`)
- **Runs project benchmarks** to measure size/performance before/after
- **Extracts metrics** from benchmark output (code size, cycle count, etc.)
- **Allows customization** — you can provide problem and approach strings to override auto-detection

## When NOT to Use

This skill is **only** for optimization commits. Use regular git commit for:
- Bug fixes (unless they're performance-related)
- Refactoring without size/performance gains
- Documentation changes
- Adding new features

## Required Parameters (optional)

- `problem` — Brief explanation of what was suboptimal (auto-detected from function changes if omitted)
- `approach` — Description of the optimization technique (auto-detected from git diff if omitted)

The benchmark result is **always** captured automatically.

## Integration with Your Project

This skill is designed for Picolibc RISC-V libm work:
- Looks for Makefile `compare` target to run benchmarks
- Falls back to `commit-size` script if available
- Parses QEMU output or size tool output to extract metrics
- Works with both `-s` assembly files and C implementations in `hardfloat/`

input.command:
pwd && git add andes_dpexlog.s && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
optimize: andes_dpexlog.s factor duplicate __clzsi2 scaffolds into helper

Problem:
Two identical 15-instruction push/call/pop blocks around __clzsi2 (at
the .LL2 and .LAret paths) shared no code, duplicating 30 instructions
of callee-save scaffolding.

Approach:
Factor into local helper .Ldo_clzsi2 that takes arg in x15, preserves
x10-x14 and ra, returns clz result in x15. Each callsite becomes 2
instructions (mv + call) instead of 15.

Result:
andes_dpexlog.o .text: 634 -> 624 bytes (-10 bytes)
make test-log, test-log10, test-pow: all PASS

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

input.command:
git add andes_fpexlog.s && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
optimize: andes_fpexlog.s factor duplicate __clzsi2 scaffolds into helper

Problem:
Two 16-17 instruction push/call/pop scaffolds around __clzsi2 (at .Le1
and .Li9 paths) shared no code, duplicating callee-save overhead.

Approach:
Factor into local helper .Ldo_clzsi2_f using x17 as input register
(avoids x10-x15 conflict), preserves x10-x15 and ra, result in x19.
Callsites become 2 instructions (mv x17 + call) instead of 16-17.

Result:
andes_fpexlog.o .text: 656 -> 644 bytes (-12 bytes)
make test-logf, test-log10f, test-powf: all PASS

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text: 402 → 348 = **-54 bytes**. Excellent - largest C-tier win. Commit:

input.command:
git add andes_dpexexp.s && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
optimize: andes_dpexexp.s factor duplicate __gf_sigmax call blocks

Problem:
Two 23-instruction save+call+restore sequences for __gf_sigmax (one
using expcoo table, one using expcon) were fully duplicated, differing
only in the la x14,<table> instruction.

Approach:
Hoist sw x14 + la x14,<table> before each path's branch; share the
21-instruction common body (.Lshared_sigmax). Both paths fall through
to .Le4. The original tail .Le4 is eliminated.

Result:
andes_dpexexp.o .text: 402 -> 348 bytes (-54 bytes)
make test-exp, test-cosh, test-sinh, test-tanh, test-pow: all PASS

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

message.content: will this optimization slow down the performance cycle?

content.text:
Yes, each change adds a small overhead:

**C1/C2 (`__clzsi2` helpers)**
- Each callsite now executes: `mv` + `call` (branch) + 18 helper instructions + `ret` (branch) = ~20 instructions + 2 branch penalties
- Original inline path: 15 instructions, no branches
- Net overhead: ~5 instructions + 2 branch penalties per `__clzsi2` invocation

**C3 (`__gf_sigmax` shared body)**
- Path 1 (expcoo): adds 1 `j .Lshared_sigmax` branch that wasn't there
- Path 2 (expcon): adds 1 extra instruction (`sw x14` hoisted out)

**In practice: negligible**

Both overhead sources are dwarfed by what surrounds them:
- `__clzsi2` is called once per `log`/`logf` invocation on a denormal input (rare path). The rest of the log computation (exdiv, exmul, sigmax polynomial) costs hundreds of cycles.
- The `__gf_sigmax` call in dpexexp evaluates an 8-term polynomial; 1 extra branch before it is noise.

If you were optimizing a tight inner loop, this trade-off would matter. Here the code is in the slow path of scalar transcendentals where function-call overhead already dominates.
